coredata.dc:title: Self-assembled cauliflower-like pyrite-S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots as free-standing anode with high conductivity and biocompatibility for bioelectricity production
coredata.dc:creator: Farahmand Habibi, Maryam
Arvand, Majid
Schröder, Uwe
Sohrabnezhad, Shabnam
coredata.dc:description: Ferrous disulfide (FeS2) decorated S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N GQDs) (FeS2-S, N GQDs), were synthesized using a facile approach involving a straight hydrothermal method and tested as bioanode in acetate-feeding microbial fuel cells (MFCs) based on a mixed bacterial culture with superior performances. The physical characterizations FeS2-S, N GQDs corroborate the unique configuration that is desirable for bacteria immobilization and effective electrolyte transport. The electrochemical measurements prove the superior performance of the bifunctional electrocatalysis of FeS2-S, N GQDs for the conversion of biomass into electricity through bacteria. Significantly, at a steady-state of the power generation, the acetate-feeding MFC with FeS2-S, N GQDs as activated biomass anode delivers a maximum power density 1128 ± 9 mW/m2, which is 48% and 76% higher than the one using S, N GQDs (585 ± 7 mW/m2) and carbon cloth (265 ± 3 mW/m2) as anode electrocatalyst. With these merits, the bioanode facilitates the activity towards redox reactions of cytochromes in bacteria and exhibits higher maximum power density and stronger stability than the conventional carbon cloth. This report provides a new concept of anode electrocatalyst fabrication for the application of MFC in electricity generation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FeS2-S
N co-doped graphene quantum dots
Microbial fuel cells
Bifunctional electrocatalyst
Activated anode
Mixed culture bacteria

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The structure and morphology of FeS2-S, N GQDs
Electrochemical properties of FeS2-S, N GQDs electrode
Electrochemical characteristics of the as-prepared samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are highly grateful for the financial support from the Iranian National Science Foundation and post-graduate office of Guilan University. The authors gratefully acknowledge Isaac Rivera Alvarez for his advice on microbial fuel cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the cauliflower-like FeS2–S, N GQDs and working mechanism of FeS2–S, N GQDs-based MFC.
XRD patterns of the as-obtained S, N GQDs and cauliflower-like FeS2–S, N GQDs samples.
FESEM images of (a–a″) CC and (b–b″) FeS2–S, N GQDs at different magnifications.
TEM images of (a) S, N GQDs and (b, c) FeS2–S, N GQDs; (d–h) EDS detecting of FeS2–S, N GQDs.
(a) Cyclic voltammograms and (b) Nyquist curves of FeS2–S, N GQDs, S, N GQDs and CC in 0.1 mol/L KCl containing 5 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−.
(a) Polarization curves and (b) power density curves of FeS2–S, N GQDs, S, N GQDs and CC; (c) MFC performance with different anodes, where the voltage outputs were examined at 1000 Ω.

Cyclic voltammograms of anodic biofilms with different catalysts under an (a) sufficient substrate supply (turnover) and (b) without substrate supply (nonturnover) (scan rates: 5 mV/s); Inset: cyclic voltammograms of different electrodes before inoculation. (c) Schematic illustration on charge transfer mechanism between bacteria and FeS2–S, N GQDs anode of MFC.
Tafel plots of the FeS2–S, N GQDs, S, N GQDs and CC anodes.
FESEM images of biofilm adhered to (a) FeS2–S, N GQDs, (b) S, N GQDs and (c) CC anodes.

head.ce:title: Self-assembled cauliflower-like pyrite-S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots as free-standing anode with high conductivity and biocompatibility for bioelectricity production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Guilan, Namjoo Street, 1914–41335 Rasht, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Guilan, Namjoo Street, 1914–41335 Rasht, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental and Sustainable Chemistry, Technische Universität Braunschweig, Hagenring 30, 38106 Braunschweig, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental and Sustainable Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental and Sustainable Chemistry, Technische Universität Braunschweig, Hagenring 30, 38106 Braunschweig, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ferrous disulfide (FeS2) decorated S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S, N GQDs) (FeS2-S, N GQDs), were synthesized using a facile approach involving a straight hydrothermal method and tested as bioanode in acetate-feeding microbial fuel cells (MFCs) based on a mixed bacterial culture with superior performances. The physical characterizations FeS2-S, N GQDs corroborate the unique configuration that is desirable for bacteria immobilization and effective electrolyte transport. The electrochemical measurements prove the superior performance of the bifunctional electrocatalysis of FeS2-S, N GQDs for the conversion of biomass into electricity through bacteria. Significantly, at a steady-state of the power generation, the acetate-feeding MFC with FeS2-S, N GQDs as activated biomass anode delivers a maximum power density 1128 ± 9 mW/m2, which is 48% and 76% higher than the one using S, N GQDs (585 ± 7 mW/m2) and carbon cloth (265 ± 3 mW/m2) as anode electrocatalyst. With these merits, the bioanode facilitates the activity towards redox reactions of cytochromes in bacteria and exhibits higher maximum power density and stronger stability than the conventional carbon cloth. This report provides a new concept of anode electrocatalyst fabrication for the application of MFC in electricity generation.

ce:section.ce:para: Our planet is facing challenges regarding the security of water and energy sources. Nonrenewable energy sources and environmental pollution have posed serious threats to our lives [1,2]. To solve these issues, researchers have gone in quest of sustainable renewable sources such as geothermal, tides, hydropower, rain, biomass, wind and sunlight [3]. Nowadays, one of the most significant renewable energy sources is biomass, which accounts for about 10% of the total primary energy supply worldwide [4]. Despite being such a useful resource, a considerable amount of biomass required is consumed through improper use, such as cooking and heating via open fires. Therefore, many researchers are looking for a new way to convert this green energy source into clean and sustainable fuel [5–8]. Among different strategies, microbial fuel cells (MFCs) which use the metabolic activity of bacteria to convert organic compounds from biomass to electricity, has drawn particular attention to its benefits including the use of waste as a green fuel and the reduction of dissolved oxygen amount required for aerobe loading of the wastewater with reducing the greenhouse gas effect [9–11]. In addition, MFCs can turn about 50 to 90% of energy from the break-down of various organic substances into sustainable green electricity with the help of living organisms [12]. In spite of the impressive progress regarding the MFCs application in the fields of electricity production, self-powered biosensors and wastewater treatment [13,14]; the main bottleneck of MFCs is the low output power density owing to microorganism culture, operating temperature, internal resistance, anodic and cathodic electrocatalysts, organic substrate concentration, reactor configuration and inadequate bacterial cells loading on anode [15,16].

Among numerous factors that largely impact the performance of bioelectrochemical fuel cells, considerations in the selection of ideal anode materials are the critical parts that control the comprehensive activity of MFCs [17–19]. The most ideal anode for MFCs must provide some clear advantages. First of all, the anode electrode should has the ability to provide a carrier for bacterial cells growth, easy removal of waste products and space availability for microorganisms to attach firmly to the surface for facile transport besides high biocompatibility and effective immobilization. Second, an ideal anode electrode should act as a supreme current collector from all regions of the anode to simplify the electron transfer released from the living microorganisms [20–24].

To date, carbonaceous electrodes (carbon paper (CP), carbon cloth (CC), activated carbon (AC), reticulated vitrified carbon (RVC), carbon foam (CF) and graphite rod) have been widely investigated and most extensively used as MFC anodes [25]. In spite of its low cost, high chemical stability and excellent electrical conductivity of carbonaceous electrodes, pure carbon-based materials have low microorganism loading capacity for bacterial colonization [26–30]. To solve these problems, modification of anode surface at a molecular scale has been conducted to reduce the electron transfer barrier and enhance the microbial immobilization [31,32].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), one of the exceptional materials for biological, environmental and energy-related applications, are carbon quantum dots subcategories [33]. These quasi-spherical structures have attracted tremendous attraction, because of good biocompatibility, ease of functionalization and processability and low cytotoxicity [33–35]. To date, doping GQDs with heteroatoms have been actively pursued, because they are emerged as one of the most promising candidates to using GQDs in material applications due to their pronounced activities and tunable properties, such as electronic properties, surface and local chemical reactivities [36,37]. The co-doping of heteroatoms used in GQDs are known to increase the performance of energy-oriented systems and promulgate more active sites for improving the electrochemical device by changing the physical and chemical properties of the electrocatalyst matter [36,38]. FeS2, in the form of marcasite or pyrite serves as one of the key energy-delivering sources for microbial growth [39]. Electricity conducting microorganisms can respire using iron(III) as insoluble and extracellular substrate. During bacterial iron reduction, sulfur acts as a mediated electron-shuttling [40–42]. Hence, anchoring of FeS2 over S, N GQDs not only improves the electrocatalytic activity, electronic conductivity and surrounded area of electrode substrate but also may enrich diverse bacterial species on the solid electrode and increases biocompatibility.

To the best of our knowledge, we engineered the first time, novel cauliflower-like FeS2-S, N GQDs by facile and one-step hydrothermal synthesis and investigated its MFC performance for promoting electricity generation and energy recycle. Herein, biocompatible S, N GQDs, decorated with FeS2 were offered as “designed additives” to cover the CC surface. The hybrid material was used as acetate-feeding MFC mixed culture anode to facilitate electron transfer and better output power density. The structural, electrochemical and electricity-generating capacity analysis results would encourage researchers to discover the next generation of sustainable bioenergy-storage systems with a supreme electrocatalytic activity using the hetero-growth and assembly of co-doping of heteroatoms/transition metal sulfides.

FeS2-S, N GQDs were prepared through a facile, straightforward one-step and effective hydrothermal method. In short, 0.675 g of iron(III) chloride, 0.2 g of thiourea and 1.0 g of citric acid was mixed with 20 mL of deionized water at room temperature under mild magnetic stirring. Next, the aforementioned homogeneous solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon autoclave reactor. Subsequently, a piece of CC (approximately 2 cm × 2 cm) which was pre-cleared by acetone for 12 h and then in 2 mol/L HCl for 4 h was soaked in the aforesaid solution, followed by heating the sealed autoclave in a dry heat (Four) at 160 °C for 8 h to form the FeS2-S, N GQDs. Finally, when the reaction was completed, the coated CC was washed using distilled water and was then dried overnight at room temperature. S, N GQDs like to FeS2-S, N GQDs was obtained without adding iron(III) chloride to the hydrothermal reaction.

X-ray diffractometer (XRD) data of FeS2-S, N GQDs and S, N GQDs were saved by Philips PW1730 XRD generator made in the Netherlands with an X-ray source of CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) to verify the crystalline structure. To investigate the element composition and surface morphology of materials of the samples, transmission electron microscope (USA, 200 kV, JEM-2100F, JEOL, TEM) and field emission scanning electron microscope (Tescan Mira III, FESEM) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscope made in the Czech Republic were applied, respectively.

In this study, the experiments were fulfilled by cubic dual chambers MFCs with an inner volumetric capacity of 50 mL and equal dimensions (4 cm × 4.5 cm × 5.5 cm) for each chamber. Two cuboid compartments were separated from each other using Nafion 117 (DuPont Inc., USA) as a proton exchange membrane. Prior to application, the Nafion 117 membrane was etched with 3% of boiling H2O2 and treated with boiling deionized water, 0.5 mol/L of boiling H2SO4 and finally rinsed in boiling deionized water again [43]. The FeS2-S, N GQDs and S, N GQDs acted as modified anodes and CC was used as the control and cathode electrode. The anaerobic compartment (anodic chamber) of biofuel cell was inoculated using active sludge (Bandar Anzali Wastewater Treatment Plant, Iran) to run MFCs. Inoculation of MFCs with active sludge was controlled as 50% (v/v) of the anolyte solution. The anode medium including 100 mmol/L of phosphate buffer (pH = 7) contained the following ammonium chloride (0.31 g/L); sodium dihydrogen phosphate dehydrate (19.88 g/L); disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (11 g/L); potassium chloride (0.13 g/L) and a trace-element solution (12.5 mL) including 0.5 g/L nicotinic acid, 1 g/L sodium chloride, 0.5 g/L manganese sulfate monohydrate, 3 g/L magnesium sulfate, 0.1 g/L ferrous sulfate heptahydrate, 0.025 g/L sodium tungstate dihydrate, 0.025 g/L sodium molybdate, 0.01 g/L boric acid, 0.01 g/L aluminum potassium sulfate dodecahydrate, 0.13 g/L zinc chloride, 0.1 g/L cobalt chloride hexahydrate, 0.024 g/L nickel chloride hexahydrate, 0.01 g/L copper sulfate pentahydrate, 0.1 g/L calcium chloride dihydrate, vitamin solution (5 mL) and sodium acetate (1 g/L) as a nutrient solution [44]. The anaerobic compartment was continuously sparged with N2 for approximately 20 min to eliminate the dissolved oxygen. The aerobic compartment (cathodic chamber) was loaded with 50 mmol/L potassium ferricyanide and 100 mmol/L potassium phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7). The MFCs were operated in the fed-batch mode condition.

The cell voltage data points across an external loading resistor (1000 Ω) were saved every 30 min by an online data logger device at ambient temperature. When the output voltage dropped to less than 50 mV, 80% of medium solution was refreshed with a new solution. To collect the polarization curves, the anode and the cathode were connected through a changeable external resistor from 100 to 0.1 KΩ at every 20 min intervals after three steady and repeatable voltage cycles. The measured voltage was converted to an electric current using Ohm’s law equation:
(1)
I
e l e c t r i c
c u r r e n t , A
=
V
o u t p u t
v o l t a g e , V
R
e x t e r n a l
r e s i s t a n c e , Ω

The produced output power was computed based on the following equation:
(2)
P
o u t p u t
p o w e r , W
= V
o u t p u t
v o l t a g e , V
× I
( e l e c t r i c
c u r r e n t , A )
To normalize the current and power output, they were divided to anode nominal geometric area projection and were then named current density and power density, respectively.

The FeS2-S, N GQDs, S, N GQDs and CC performances were assessed using cyclic voltammetry (CV), Linear polarization and frequency response analyzer impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with an Autolab PGSTAT 30 potentiostat/galvanostat analyzer (Eco Chemie BV, Utrecht, the Netherlands) in a classical three-electrode configuration. The parameters were as follows: in regard of linear polarization, start potential = −0.08 mV, stop potential = 0.08 mV, scan rate = 1 mV/s; as for EIS, first applied frequency = 0.01 Hz, last applied frequency = 100 KHz, potential amplitude = 10 mV; for CV in turnover and non-turnover situation, start potential = −0.6 mV, stop potential = 0.6 mV, scan rate = 5 mV/s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure and morphology of FeS2-S, N GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The facile synthetic route of FeS2-S, N GQDs hybrid is graphically represented in Fig. 1 . XRD patterns of the as-prepared MFCs anode materials in the present work are illustrated in Fig. 2 . Based on the crystalline pattern peaks at about 2θ = 23.4–24.6, the presence of the broad peak corresponds to S, N GQDs [35]. The broad reflection peak revels a disordered amorphous and the nature of the carbon phase which is ascribed to the presence of, oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur-containing groups [37]. Based on the XRD pattern of the cauliflower-like FeS2 in the attendance of S, N GQDs, the reflections assigned to (111), (200), (210), (211), (220), (221), (311), (222), (023), (321) and (400) with diffraction peaks at 28.5°, 33.0°, 37.1°, 40.8°, 47.4°, 50.5°, 57.3°, 59.0°, 61.7°, 64.3° and 69.3°, completely demonstrates pyrite phase FeS2 with Fd3m space group (JCPDS card no. 99-0087) [45–48].

Morphological FESEM observations of the raw CC and FeS2-S, N GQDs obtained after the hydrothermal process are clearly presented in Fig. 3 . Original CC shares a relatively compact and smooth structure (Fig. 3(a-a″)). Interestingly, the FeS2-S, N GQDs reveal cauliflower-like structures that are uniformly anchored on the surface of woven carbon microfibers (Fig. 3(b-b″)). Magnified FESEM image of the composite shows each bud in the cauliflower-like structure is interconnected small-sized nanoparticles. Therefore, cauliflower-like FeS2-S, N GQDs enhance the contact accessible surface area between the cauliflower-like as-papered electrode and microorganism and create numerous active sites exposed to bacterial colonization that can help improve the biocompatibility.

More information from the interior structure of S, N GQDs and FeS2-S, N GQDs was obtained using TEM. Fig. 4 (a) shows the typical spherical morphology of S, N GQDs, with an average size of less than 10 nm. TEM images of the resulting FeS2-S, N GQDs clearly demonstrate the cauliflower-like architecture of composite (Fig. 4(b, c)).

Furthermore, EDX elemental spot reflects that FeS2-S, N GQDs merely contains S, N, O, C and Fe atoms (Fig. 4(d, inset)). The EDX mapping of FeS2-S, N GQDs reveals the distribution of S, N, O, C and Fe elements over the whole composite on the CC in the absence of any possible impurities and residues (Fig. 4(d–h)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of FeS2-S, N GQDs electrode
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the electron transfer kinetics and the electrochemical properties of CC, S, N GQDs and FeS2-S, N GQDs electrodes, CV and EIS techniques were applied to evaluate the substrate-electrodes interactions. The CV measurements of the prepared electrodes in a three-electrode system containing 5.0 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in supporting electrolyte (KCl 0.1 mol/L) were systematically conducted. The CV profile peak current of the CC that is smaller compared to the as-prepared S, N GQDs electrode proves weak electron transfer rate and low available surface area (Fig. 5 (a)). Furthermore, the FeS2-S, N GQDs reflect more current density response compared to S, N GQDs offering that the FeS2-S, N GQDs possess better electron transfer rate, more electroactive sites and more convenient ion/electron transport. As a result, FeS2-S, N GQDs lead to a higher electrochemical activity which plays a fundamental role in electric reactions and controlling the electronic capacitance existing in the as-prepared electrode.

The diffusion property and conductivity of the FeS2-S, N GQDs were validated by EIS along with S, N GQDs and commonly used CC. As it is evident in Fig. 5(b), at high-frequency range FeS2-S, N GQDs and S, N GQDs displayed a very small semicircle compared to a CC, owning to high electronic conduction and fast rate of electron transfer at the interface between as-papered electrodes and [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− electrolyte. Moreover, the R s value of the FeS2-S, N GQDs highlighted less resistance for the electrolyte-electrode material interaction ascribed to the rest of the electrodes. As shown in the low-frequency region, the Warburg impedance of FeS2-S, N GQDs extend steeper than that of S, N GQDs and CC, which implies a facilitated diffusional process and an excellent mass transfer process at the FeS2-S, N GQDs.

The microbial electricity generation of FeS2-S, N GQDs as an anode was investigated in a stacked dual-chamber MFC with a comparison of S, N GQDs and CC. The active sludge and acetate solution (1 g/L) were applied as an inoculated solution to anodic chamber of the MFC reactor. After 25 days of inoculation with batch mode operation, the polarization and power output plots were assayed by varying the external resistor (Fig. 6 ). With regard to polarization profile, the voltage with as-prepared MFC using the FeS2-S, N GQDs as anode declines slowly, while the external loading decreases and represents the largest current density in comparison with S, N GQDs and CC, suggesting that FeS2-S, N GQDs has small polarization (Fig. 6(a)). Clearly, the FeS2 and the S, N GQDs are useful for the microbial electricity generation improvement. Observably, the FeS2-S, N GQDs anode generated electricity with a maximum power density of 1128 ± 9 mW/m2 which is 48% and 76% higher than the outputs of the S, N GQDs (585 ± 7 mW/m2) and CC (265 ± 3 mW/m2), respectively (Fig. 6(b)). Eventually, the FeS2 facilitates the redox reactions in microorganisms because of enhancing the electrocatalytic activity. Moreover, the S, N GQDs improves the electronic conductivity of the CC that is essential for the redox reactions between biofilm and anode electrode. It is deduced that FeS2-S, N GQDs possesses more opportunities to interact with bacteria cell membrane for efficient extracellular electron transfer because of the synergic effect of FeS2 and S, N GQDs.

Fig. 6(c) depicts the discharge curves of FeS2-S, N GQDs and CC under a fixed -load external resistance of 1000 Ω. Compared to a CC, a higher voltage output and a shorter start-up time were clearly observed for the MFC which was equipped with FeS2-S, N GQDs electrode. At the post step of each batch, the output voltage decreased slowly as a result of the electron donor (acetate) depletion, however, it could regenerate immediately after replacing of fresh electron donor, suggesting the supreme durability and stability of FeS2-S, N GQDs for long-time running.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of the as-prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further elucidate the extracellular electron transfer process, the designation of substrate depletion (non-turnover) and maximum voltage output (turnover), CV was carried out. Generally, appropriate information about excreted electron shuttles as a mediate and passed electrons through bacteria wall contact with the active substance (anode electrode) in extracellular electron transfer can be obtained by CV examination [49–51]. Fig. 7 (a, inset), displays the cyclic voltammograms of naked CC, S, N GQDs and FeS2-S, N GQDs electrodes before the formation of biofilm with mixed culture. Under the conditions without inoculation, no obvious redox peaks are observed upon mentioned electrodes, suggesting that all anodes have no electrochemical redox behavior toward the oxidation of acetate. A pair of redox peaks were shown in the S, N GQDsandFeS2-S, N GQDs anodes, corresponding to the electroactive biofilms grown on electrodes to catalytic oxidation of sodium acetate (Fig. 7(a)) [32,52]. It is noted that the CV plot of the CC shows just a reduction peak in the turnover condition. The electroactive biofilm on the FeS2-S, N GQDs generated a maximum current density of 0.93 ± 0.02 A/m, which was 1.6 and 9.3 fold that of S, N GQDs (0.59 ± 0.02 A/m) and CC (0.11 ± 0.01 A/m), demonstrating its superior catalytic activity and conductivity for bioelectrogenesis. At the non-turnover state (Fig. 7(b)), the FeS2-S, N GQDs displayed one pair of distinct redox peaks with the potential of −0.28 V (anodic peak) and −0.37 V (cathodic peak). The obtained result with the CV scanning may be attributed to the outer membrane cytochromes (OMCs) or binding of flavins and OMCs and suggesting the mixed exoelectrogens on FeS2–S, N GQDs and extracellular microbial electron transfer during power generation [52–55]. In addition, the anodic potential of 0.10 V observed in the voltammogram may be originated from some unidentified bacterial metabolites [56]. Taken together, the introduction of S, N GQDs and FeS2 could be regarded as the essential factors in the increase of biofilm loading on FeS2-S, N GQDs anode. Based on the detected oxidation peak and its potential corresponding proteins, the electron pathway involved in the MFC may be expressed as shown in Fig. 7c.

More information about the bio-electrocatalytic activity and extracellular electron transfer efficiency can be extracted by Tafel plots. According to the splines in Fig. 8 , the i 0 (exchange current density) value observed for the FeS2-S, N GQDs, S, N GQDs and CC in a fresh anolyte were approximately 2.22 × 10−6, 1.94 × 10−6 and 1.14 × 10−6 A/cm, which were inconsistent with the power and voltage output in the stabilized operation period. In addition, the values of the b a (Tafel slope) were about 0.016, 0.020 and 0.031 V/dec for the FeS2-S, N GQDs, S, N GQDs and CC anodes, respectively. In the light of these results, FeS2-S, N GQDs possess the higher bio-electrocatalytic activity and electron discharge capability than S, N GQDs and CC.

Bactria, as the palpitating heart of green and sustainable energy technology, need the appropriate conditions to perform their task in the best way possible. After a one-month discharge operation, the bacteria-hosting capability on the FeS2-S, N GQDs electrode was characterized by FESEM, in comparison with that of S, N GQDS and CC electrodes (Fig. 9 ). It appears that the bacteria formed a multilayered confluent biofilm on FeS2-S, N GQDs (Fig. 9(a)). In contrast, in the absence of FeS2, both surface sides of the S, N GQDs and CC anodes were covered by fewer bacterial cells and much of the substrates were not covered by bacteria (Fig. 9(b, c)). Furthermore, microorganisms grow densely on FeS2-S, N GQDs surface to form a thick biofilm due to its positively charged surface, superhydrophilicity and suitable roughness, obviously revealing that the cauliflower-like FeS2-S, N GQDs have higher biological behavior for better bacteria-hosting capability.

To conclude, we represented the production of a sustainable MFC containing an electroactive FeS2-S, N GQDs anode through a facile hydrothermal process. The as-prepared anode was engineered by merging the FeS2-S, N GQDs composite on the surface of the CC. The introduction of S, N GQDs boosted the interior surface of the substrate which favored bacterial loading capacity and subsequent electroactive biofilm formation. The existence of FeS2 not only profits bacterial attachment but also enriches the electrically conductive bacteria on the solid substrate. In addition, the FeS2-S, N GQDs composite also persuades surface interaction with the bacterial biofilm. These phenomena greatly promote easier extracellular electron transfer from electrically conductive bacteria to the electrode surface, so impressively enhancing the electricity generation duration of MFC. As a result, this aforesaid multicomponent hybrid material as anode for the MFC reactor delivered maximum power density of 1128 ± 9 mW/m2, which is substantially more than that of the raw CC. Therefore, the making of FeS2-S, N GQDs on CC and its sustainable green energy production from wastewater postulate a competitive and promising way to turn waste into profit.

Maryam Farahmand Habibi: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Majid Arvand: Supervision, Methodology, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Uwe Schröder: Methodology, Validation. Shabnam Sohrabnezhad: Methodology, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are highly grateful for the financial support from the Iranian National Science Foundation and post-graduate office of Guilan University. The authors gratefully acknowledge Isaac Rivera Alvarez for his advice on microbial fuel cells.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The integrated processes for wastewater treatment based on the principle of microbial fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu L, Zhao Y, Doherty L, Hu Y, Hao X. The integrated processes for wastewater treatment based on the principle of microbial fuel cells. Criti Rev Env Sci Tech 2015;46:60−91.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microbial fuel cell: a bioelectrochemical system that converts waste to watts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Das D. Microbial fuel cell: a bioelectrochemical system that converts waste to watts, 1st ed. New York: Springer; 2018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Global renewable energy resources and use in 2050

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Moriarty P, Honnery D. Global renewable energy resources and use in 2050. In: Letcher T, editor. Managing in global warming, Amestrdam: Elsevier; 2019, p. 221−235.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A miniaturized microbial fuel cell with three-dimensional graphene macroporous scaffold anode demonstrating a record power density of over 10000 W m−3

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ren H, Tian H, Gardner C, Ren T, Chae J. A miniaturized microbial fuel cell with three-dimensional graphene macroporous scaffold anode demonstrating a record power density of over 10000 W m−3. Nanoscale 2016;8:3539−3547.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conversion of wastes into bioelectricity and chemicals by using microbial electrochemical technologies

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conjugated oligoelectrolytes increase power generation in E. coli microbial fuel cells
